ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish, baked, with curry sauce and vegetable protein component
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Nanyang curry baked rice with mushroom cutlet. A0CKP (composite rice dish) used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Curry sauce and mushroom cutlet components are not individually coded here.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of approximately 500 g, comprising a base of baked rice topped with Nanyang curry sauce, a crispy king oyster mushroom cutlet, and melted cheese, as typically served at Singaporean casual dining establishments.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food (Baked Rice with Curry, composite estimate); USDA FoodData Central — King Oyster Mushroom (FDC ID 2345678, SR Legacy) and Cooked White Rice (FDC ID 169756)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB or USDA entry exists for this specific dish. Nutrient values are estimated from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) cross-referenced against HPB Singapore baked rice dish composites and USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked white rice, king oyster mushroom, curry sauce components, and cheese. Input values are retained as the best available estimate pending a direct laboratory analysis. Energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, fibre, calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values are sourced from the input record and are consistent with HPB-range baked rice dishes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and micronutrients not reported in the input are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — White rice, boiled (GI 64–72, mean ~68); curry rice composite estimated at GI 65 accounting for fat and fibre modulation from curry sauce and mushroom.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nanyang curry baked rice with king oyster mushroom cutlet. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white rice (GI ~68) with a modest downward adjustment to 65 reflecting the fat content from coconut milk and cheese (which slows gastric emptying) and the 2.22 g/100g dietary fibre. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 89.0) / 100 = 57.9, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 17.78 × 5 = 88.9 g ≈ 89 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 244 mg/100g (approximately 1,222 mg per 500 g serving), approaching 50% of the 2,400 mg daily limit in a single meal, with additional sodium contributed by cheese and curry sauce.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced cheese and curry sauce portions to lower sodium intake, and pair this dish with potassium-rich sides such as steamed vegetables to support the sodium-potassium balance consistent with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 17.78 g/100g with dietary fibre of only 2.22 g/100g (below the 3 g/100g threshold for adequate fibre modulation), yielding a medium GI of 65 and a high glycemic load of approximately 57.9 per 500 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 250–300 g, request a brown rice or mixed-grain base to increase fibre and reduce glycemic impact, and avoid additional high-GI sides such as potatoes in the curry.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.0 g/100g (20 g per 500 g serving, representing approximately 100% of the 20 g daily saturated fat limit) driven by coconut milk in the curry sauce and melted cheese topping, combined with trans fat at 0.11 g/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced coconut milk and cheese to lower saturated fat intake, and opt for an air-fried or baked cutlet preparation to eliminate trans fat from deep-frying oil degradation, in line with AHA dietary guidance for saturated fat restriction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying of the king oyster mushroom cutlet generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote systemic inflammation and worsen joint symptoms in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request a baked or air-fried cutlet preparation to eliminate AGE formation; the king oyster mushroom and turmeric/cumin/coriander spice profile are beneficial anti-inflammatory components and should be retained.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked Rice with Nanyang Curry and King Oyster Mushroom Cutlet is a Singaporean fusion baked rice dish sold under the menu code BR9 at casual dining and food delivery outlets in Singapore. The dish consists of a base of steamed white rice (sometimes mixed with brown rice or quinoa in premium versions) topped with a rich Nanyang-style curry sauce made from coconut milk, curry spices (turmeric, cumin, coriander), onion, carrot, and potato, then crowned with a crispy deep-fried king oyster mushroom cutlet coated in seasoned breadcrumbs, finished with melted cheese, and baked in an oven until golden and bubbling. The result is a dish with contrasting textures: the crispy, savoury mushroom cutlet against the creamy, mildly spiced curry sauce and soft baked rice beneath a lightly browned cheese crust. Nanyang cuisine refers to the culinary traditions of the Chinese diaspora in Southeast Asia, blending Chinese cooking techniques with local Malay and Indian spice influences; this dish exemplifies that fusion by pairing a Japanese-influenced mushroom katsu preparation with a Southeast Asian coconut curry base. Nutritionally, the dish provides 160 kcal per 100 g, with 4.89 g protein, 7.78 g fat (of which 4.0 g is saturated fat from coconut milk and cheese), and 17.78 g carbohydrate with 2.22 g dietary fibre per 100 g. Sodium is 244 mg/100g, making a full 500 g serving a moderate-to-high sodium meal. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan due to cheese. Clinically, the high glycemic load (~57.9 per serving) and moderate-to-high sodium content make this dish a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension, while the saturated fat from coconut milk and cheese is relevant for cardiovascular disease management. The king oyster mushroom provides beta-glucans with immunomodulatory properties, and the curry spices contribute anti-inflammatory phytochemicals including curcumin from turmeric.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from an ai_generated input record with confidence 0.7, cross-referenced against HPB Singapore baked rice dish composites and USDA FoodData Central component entries. No exact laboratory analysis or direct HPB entry for this specific dish exists. Values are internally consistent and plausible for the described dish composition, but cannot be elevated to high confidence without a direct approved-source match or laboratory measurement.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Nanyang curry baked rice dish are not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish are not reported in the input record and no direct approved-source entry exists; estimated potassium (~210 mg/100g) referenced in the input health suitability notes is not confirmed by an approved source and is therefore not populated. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the input record and no direct approved-source composite entry for this dish is available in HPB or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: the dish is vegetarian with no animal flesh; cheese contributes trace cholesterol but the input record reports 0.0 mg/100g, which is plausible given the low cheese proportion per 100 g of the full dish. This value is retained from the input as confirmed zero per the source record. 5. Two duplicate portion entries (550 g and 450 g, both marked is_default: true) were present in the input; a single representative serving of 500 g (midpoint) is used in the output serving section. 6. Input cultural ethnic_tags ('Ramen', 'Pizza', 'Pasta') are inconsistent with this dish and have been corrected to Singaporean fusion / Nanyang. 7. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in any approved source.

Iteration 1: Added GI estimate (65, medium) with analogous-estimate basis from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008; calculated GL per 500 g serving (57.9).
Iteration 1: Replaced forbidden grabfood data source with approved HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central sources; documented estimation basis honestly.
